quiz_questions.question_text: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is caused by:

options.text: Excessive bile production by the liver
Chronic reflux of gastric acid into the esophagus due to lower esophageal sphincter (LES) dysfunction
Insufficient acid production in the stomach
Food allergies triggering immune responses in the esophagus

quiz_questions.explanation: GERD occurs when the lower esophageal sphincter fails to maintain adequate closure, allowing acidic gastric contents to reflux into the esophagus. The esophageal epithelium lacks the protective mucus layer found in the stomach, making it vulnerable to acid damage, leading to heartburn, inflammation, and potential complications like Barrett's esophagus.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most common causes of peptic ulcers are:

options.text: Stress and spicy foods
Helicobacter pylori infection and chronic NSAID use
Excessive bile reflux from the gallbladder
Viral infections of the stomach lining

quiz_questions.explanation: H. pylori bacteria colonize the gastric mucosa and produce urease, which weakens the protective mucosal barrier. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, reducing mucosal blood flow and bicarbonate/mucus secretion. Together, these are responsible for the vast majority of peptic ulcers.

quiz_questions.question_text: H. pylori infection is treated with:

options.text: Surgery to remove the infected portion of the stomach
Triple therapy consisting of two antibiotics plus a proton pump inhibitor (PPI)
Antacids alone taken for several months
No treatment, as the infection resolves spontaneously

quiz_questions.explanation: Standard H. pylori eradication therapy uses two antibiotics (commonly clarithromycin and amoxicillin or metronidazole) to kill the bacteria plus a PPI to reduce acid production, creating an environment that enhances antibiotic effectiveness and promotes mucosal healing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Crohn's disease differs from ulcerative colitis in that Crohn's disease:

options.text: Only affects the colon and involves only mucosal inflammation
Can affect any segment of the GI tract from mouth to anus and involves transmural (full-thickness) inflammation
Is not classified as an inflammatory bowel disease
Always resolves with dietary changes alone

quiz_questions.explanation: Crohn's disease is characterized by skip lesions (discontinuous patches of inflammation), transmural involvement affecting all layers of the bowel wall, and the potential to affect any part of the GI tract, though the terminal ileum is the most common site. This contrasts with ulcerative colitis, which is limited to continuous mucosal inflammation of the colon.

quiz_questions.question_text: Celiac disease is triggered by exposure to:

options.text: Lactose in dairy products
Gluten, a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye
Excess dietary fat
Artificial food additives and preservatives

quiz_questions.explanation: Celiac disease is an autoimmune disorder in which the ingestion of gluten (specifically the gliadin component) triggers an immune response that damages the small intestinal villi. The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet, which allows the villi to regenerate and symptoms to resolve.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ulcerative colitis is limited to which organ?

options.text: Small intestine
Colon (large intestine), with continuous mucosal inflammation typically starting at the rectum
Esophagus
Stomach and duodenum

quiz_questions.explanation: Ulcerative colitis exclusively affects the colon, with inflammation limited to the mucosa and submucosa (not transmural). It characteristically begins at the rectum and extends proximally in a continuous pattern, unlike Crohn's disease which can affect any GI segment in a discontinuous fashion.

quiz_questions.question_text: IBS (irritable bowel syndrome) is classified as a:

options.text: Structural disease with visible intestinal damage
Functional GI disorder with altered motility and visceral sensation but no structural damage
Cancerous condition requiring immediate surgery
Infectious disease caused by bacteria in the gut

quiz_questions.explanation: IBS is a functional gastrointestinal disorder characterized by abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits (diarrhea, constipation, or both) without any identifiable structural or biochemical abnormality. It is diagnosed based on symptom criteria (Rome IV criteria) after excluding organic diseases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Colorectal cancer screening is recommended to begin at age:

options.text: 60 for average-risk individuals
45 for average-risk individuals
30 for all individuals
70 for average-risk individuals

quiz_questions.explanation: Current guidelines recommend that average-risk adults begin colorectal cancer screening at age 45 using colonoscopy or other approved methods. Colonoscopy is considered the gold standard because it can both detect and remove precancerous polyps (adenomas) during the same procedure, preventing cancer development.

quiz_questions.question_text: C. difficile (C. diff) infection most commonly occurs after:

options.text: Eating contaminated food at a restaurant
Antibiotic use that disrupts the normal gut flora, allowing C. difficile overgrowth
Excessive exercise and dehydration
Chronic stress and poor sleep quality

quiz_questions.explanation: C. difficile infection (CDI) typically occurs when broad-spectrum antibiotics eliminate the normal competitive gut flora, creating an ecological niche for C. difficile spores to germinate and overgrow. The bacteria produce toxins A and B that damage the colonic mucosa, causing pseudomembranous colitis with watery diarrhea, fever, and abdominal pain.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most common treatment approach for GERD includes:

options.text: Immediate surgical fundoplication for all patients
Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) combined with lifestyle modifications such as weight loss, elevating the head of bed, and avoiding trigger foods
Antibiotics to treat the underlying infection
No treatment is available for GERD

quiz_questions.explanation: PPIs (such as omeprazole) are the mainstay pharmacological treatment for GERD because they irreversibly inhibit the hydrogen-potassium ATPase pump on parietal cells, dramatically reducing gastric acid secretion. Lifestyle modifications include weight loss, avoiding late-night meals, elevating the head of the bed, and limiting alcohol, caffeine, and fatty foods.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Barrett's esophagus?

options.text: An acute bacterial infection of the esophageal mucosa
A precancerous condition in which the normal squamous epithelium of the esophagus undergoes metaplasia to columnar (intestinal-type) epithelium due to chronic acid exposure from GERD
A congenital narrowing of the esophagus present from birth
Inflammation of the esophagus caused by food allergies

quiz_questions.explanation: Barrett's esophagus is a complication of long-standing GERD in which chronic acid injury causes the stratified squamous epithelium of the distal esophagus to be replaced by columnar epithelium with goblet cells (intestinal metaplasia). This metaplastic change is a premalignant condition that increases the risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma, requiring regular endoscopic surveillance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is diverticulitis?

options.text: A type of colon cancer that develops from polyps
Inflammation or infection of diverticula, which are small outpouchings that develop in weakened areas of the colon wall
A liver disease caused by chronic alcohol consumption
An allergic reaction affecting the esophageal lining

quiz_questions.explanation: Diverticula are small sac-like herniations of the mucosa through the muscularis of the colon wall (diverticulosis). When these pouches become inflamed or infected, the condition is called diverticulitis. It is common in older adults and is associated with low-fiber diets, presenting with left lower quadrant pain, fever, and altered bowel habits.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is appendicitis?

options.text: Chronic inflammation of the cecum caused by inflammatory bowel disease
Acute inflammation of the vermiform appendix, typically caused by obstruction of its lumen, presenting with right lower quadrant pain at McBurney's point
A benign growth on the appendix that requires no treatment
An infection of the ascending colon caused by bacteria

quiz_questions.explanation: Appendicitis occurs when the lumen of the appendix is obstructed (often by a fecalith or lymphoid hyperplasia), leading to bacterial overgrowth, distension, ischemia, and inflammation. Classic presentation includes periumbilical pain that migrates to the right lower quadrant (McBurney's point), with fever and rebound tenderness. It is a surgical emergency because a ruptured appendix can cause life-threatening peritonitis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are hemorrhoids?

options.text: Precancerous polyps found during colonoscopy
Swollen, inflamed veins in the rectal and anal area caused by increased venous pressure
Ulcerations in the stomach lining from acid exposure
Diverticula that have formed in the rectum

quiz_questions.explanation: Hemorrhoids are dilated, swollen veins of the hemorrhoidal plexus in the anal canal. Internal hemorrhoids arise above the dentate line and are covered by columnar epithelium, while external hemorrhoids arise below the dentate line and are covered by squamous epithelium. They are commonly caused by chronic straining, constipation, pregnancy, and prolonged sitting.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)' mean?

options.text: A single condition that only affects the stomach
A group of chronic inflammatory conditions of the GI tract, primarily including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, involving immune-mediated intestinal inflammation
Another name for irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
An acute food poisoning episode that resolves within days

quiz_questions.explanation: IBD is an umbrella term for chronic, relapsing inflammatory conditions of the gastrointestinal tract, primarily Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. Unlike IBS (a functional disorder), IBD involves genuine immune-mediated tissue damage visible on endoscopy and biopsy, and it carries a risk of complications including strictures, fistulas, and colorectal cancer.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'portal hypertension' mean?

options.text: High blood pressure in the systemic arterial circulation
Abnormally elevated blood pressure in the hepatic portal venous system, most commonly caused by cirrhosis obstructing blood flow through the liver
Low blood pressure in the veins draining the intestines
Increased pressure in the pulmonary arteries affecting digestion

quiz_questions.explanation: Portal hypertension occurs when resistance to blood flow through the liver is increased, most commonly due to cirrhosis. The elevated pressure in the portal system causes blood to find alternative routes (portosystemic anastomoses), leading to complications such as esophageal varices, caput medusae, hemorrhoids, splenomegaly, and ascites.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'steatorrhea' mean?

options.text: Excessive blood in the stool
The passage of abnormally fatty, foul-smelling, pale stools that float, indicating fat malabsorption
Chronic constipation with hard, dry stools
Diarrhea caused by a bacterial infection

quiz_questions.explanation: Steatorrhea literally means 'fat flowing' and refers to the excretion of excess fat in the stool. It occurs when fat digestion or absorption is impaired, such as in pancreatic insufficiency (lack of lipase), bile salt deficiency (liver disease, ileal resection), or mucosal disease (celiac disease). The stools are characteristically greasy, pale, bulky, and foul-smelling.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'fecal occult blood test (FOBT)' mean?

options.text: A blood test that measures liver enzyme levels to detect liver disease
A screening test that detects hidden (not visible to the naked eye) blood in the stool, which may indicate colorectal cancer or other GI bleeding sources
A stool culture to identify bacterial pathogens causing diarrhea
A urine test that detects blood from urinary tract infections

quiz_questions.explanation: The FOBT is a non-invasive screening test that uses chemical reagents or antibodies to detect small amounts of blood in the stool that are invisible to the naked eye (occult means hidden). A positive FOBT warrants further investigation, typically with a colonoscopy, to identify the source of bleeding, which may include polyps, colorectal cancer, ulcers, or inflammatory conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a peptic ulcer?

options.text: A cancerous growth in the stomach or duodenal wall
An open sore or erosion in the mucosal lining of the stomach (gastric ulcer) or duodenum (duodenal ulcer) caused by an imbalance between acid/pepsin and mucosal defense mechanisms
A viral infection of the small intestine causing diarrhea
Inflammation of the esophagus from chronic acid reflux

quiz_questions.explanation: A peptic ulcer is a break in the mucosal lining that extends through the muscularis mucosae, exposing the underlying tissue to gastric acid and pepsin. It results from an imbalance between aggressive factors (HCl, pepsin, H. pylori, NSAIDs) and protective factors (mucus, bicarbonate, prostaglandins, mucosal blood flow). Duodenal ulcers are more common than gastric ulcers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'metaplasia' mean in the context of GI disorders?

options.text: The spread of cancer cells from one organ to another
A reversible change in which one mature cell type is replaced by another mature cell type, often as an adaptive response to chronic irritation or injury
The normal growth and division of cells in the intestinal lining
The death of cells due to lack of blood supply

quiz_questions.explanation: Metaplasia is an adaptive cellular response to chronic stress or injury in which one differentiated cell type is replaced by another that is better suited to withstand the new environment. In Barrett's esophagus, chronic acid exposure causes the squamous epithelium to undergo intestinal metaplasia to columnar epithelium. While initially protective, metaplasia can progress to dysplasia and eventually carcinoma.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 35-year-old woman presents with chronic bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and urgency that started in her rectum and has spread to involve the entire left colon. Colonoscopy shows continuous, superficial mucosal inflammation with no skip lesions. Which condition is most consistent with these findings?

options.text: Crohn's disease
Ulcerative colitis
Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
Colorectal cancer

quiz_questions.explanation: This presentation is classic for ulcerative colitis: bloody diarrhea, beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern, with inflammation limited to the mucosa. Crohn's disease would show skip lesions, transmural inflammation, and could involve any GI segment. IBS does not cause visible mucosal inflammation or bloody stools.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 28-year-old man with known Crohn's disease presents with right lower quadrant pain, a low-grade fever, and a tender, palpable mass. Imaging reveals a fistula between two loops of small bowel. How does the transmural nature of Crohn's disease explain this complication?

options.text: The mucosal inflammation causes excessive mucus production that erodes adjacent organs
Full-thickness (transmural) inflammation can penetrate through all layers of the bowel wall, creating abnormal connections (fistulas) between bowel loops or between bowel and other organs
The skip lesions of Crohn's disease create holes between inflamed and non-inflamed segments
Crohn's disease only affects the mucosa, so the fistula must be from a different cause

quiz_questions.explanation: Unlike ulcerative colitis which is limited to the mucosa, Crohn's disease involves transmural inflammation that extends through the entire thickness of the bowel wall. This deep inflammation can penetrate the serosa and create fistulous tracts to adjacent bowel loops, bladder, skin, or vagina, and can also cause strictures and abscesses.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 62-year-old man with a long history of heartburn undergoes an upper endoscopy that reveals salmon-colored mucosa extending several centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. Biopsy confirms intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells. His gastroenterologist schedules regular surveillance endoscopies. Why is surveillance necessary?

options.text: The metaplastic tissue will definitely become cancerous within a year
Barrett's esophagus (intestinal metaplasia from chronic GERD) is a premalignant condition with an increased risk of progressing to esophageal adenocarcinoma through dysplasia
The goblet cells will spread to the stomach and cause gastric cancer
Surveillance is only performed to monitor for H. pylori infection

quiz_questions.explanation: Barrett's esophagus carries an approximately 0.5% per year risk of progressing to esophageal adenocarcinoma through the sequence of metaplasia, low-grade dysplasia, high-grade dysplasia, and then invasive carcinoma. Regular surveillance endoscopies with systematic biopsies allow early detection of dysplasia, when intervention (ablation or endoscopic resection) can prevent cancer development.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospitalized elderly patient on broad-spectrum antibiotics for pneumonia develops profuse, watery, foul-smelling diarrhea. Stool testing is positive for C. difficile toxins. The infectious disease specialist recommends stopping the offending antibiotic and starting a different antibiotic targeting C. difficile. Why did the original antibiotics cause this infection?

options.text: The antibiotics directly stimulate C. difficile bacteria to produce more toxins

The broad-spectrum antibiotics eliminated the normal protective gut flora, creating an ecological void that allowed C. difficile spores to germinate, proliferate, and produce cytotoxins that damage the colonic mucosa
The antibiotics weakened the patient's immune system, allowing C. difficile to enter from the bloodstream
C. difficile is a normal part of the gut flora that was activated by the antibiotics

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal gut microbiome provides colonization resistance against C. difficile through competition for nutrients and production of inhibitory substances. When broad-spectrum antibiotics disrupt this protective flora, C. difficile spores (which are resistant to most antibiotics) can germinate, colonize the colon, and produce toxins A and B that cause mucosal inflammation and pseudomembranous colitis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50-year-old man with known liver cirrhosis from chronic hepatitis B is rushed to the ER after vomiting a large amount of bright red blood. Emergency endoscopy reveals bleeding esophageal varices. What is the pathophysiological chain of events that led to this life-threatening complication?

options.text: Cirrhosis caused excessive bile production that eroded the esophageal blood vessels
Cirrhosis obstructed hepatic blood flow, causing portal hypertension, which forced blood through collateral veins (including esophageal veins) that dilated into fragile varices prone to rupture
Hepatitis B virus directly infected the esophageal veins, weakening their walls
The cirrhotic liver produced clotting factors that caused blood clots in the esophageal veins

quiz_questions.explanation: In cirrhosis, extensive hepatic fibrosis increases resistance to portal blood flow, causing portal hypertension. Blood backs up and is diverted through portosystemic anastomoses, including the submucosal veins of the lower esophagus. These thin-walled veins become engorged (varices) and can rupture catastrophically, causing massive upper GI bleeding with a high mortality rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 70-year-old woman presents to the ER with sudden-onset left lower quadrant pain, fever, and elevated white blood cell count. She has a history of chronic constipation and a low-fiber diet. A CT scan shows inflammation around a diverticulum in the sigmoid colon with a small contained perforation. Which diagnosis and contributing factor best explain her condition?

options.text: Colorectal cancer caused by her low-fiber diet
Acute diverticulitis, where a low-fiber diet and chronic constipation contributed to diverticula formation, and obstruction of a diverticulum caused inflammation and perforation
Ulcerative colitis flare triggered by the low-fiber diet
Acute appendicitis, since the sigmoid colon is near the appendix

quiz_questions.explanation: Low-fiber diets produce small, hard stools that require increased intraluminal pressure for propulsion, which over time causes herniation of the mucosa through weak points in the colon wall (diverticulosis). When a diverticulum becomes obstructed (by fecal material), bacteria proliferate and cause inflammation (diverticulitis), which can progress to perforation, abscess, or peritonitis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 25-year-old woman with chronic abdominal pain, bloating, and alternating diarrhea and constipation has undergone extensive testing including blood work, stool studies, and a colonoscopy, all of which are completely normal. Her symptoms worsen during periods of stress. Which diagnosis is most appropriate?

options.text: Crohn's disease in early stages before visible inflammation develops
Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), a functional disorder diagnosed by symptom criteria after excluding structural disease
Celiac disease that is not detectable on standard testing
Early-stage colorectal cancer that is too small to detect on colonoscopy

quiz_questions.explanation: IBS is a diagnosis of exclusion characterized by recurrent abdominal pain associated with defecation and changes in stool frequency or form, without identifiable structural or biochemical abnormalities. The normal test results, symptom pattern, and stress-related exacerbation are all consistent with IBS, which involves dysregulation of the gut-brain axis, visceral hypersensitivity, and altered motility.

quiz_questions.question_text: A primary care physician performs a routine fecal occult blood test (FOBT) on a 55-year-old asymptomatic male patient. The test returns positive. The physician recommends a colonoscopy. During the procedure, three polyps are found and removed. Why is this sequence of screening and intervention important?

options.text: The FOBT diagnosed colorectal cancer, and the polyps are the cancerous tumors being removed
The positive FOBT detected hidden bleeding that prompted colonoscopy, which found and removed precancerous adenomatous polyps before they could transform into colorectal cancer
The FOBT was a false positive, and the polyps are unrelated incidental findings
The FOBT detected inflammation from ulcerative colitis, and the polyps are inflammatory pseudopolyps

quiz_questions.explanation: This scenario illustrates the adenoma-carcinoma sequence in colorectal cancer prevention. The FOBT detected microscopic bleeding from polyps. Colonoscopic polypectomy removed adenomatous polyps, which are precancerous lesions that accumulate genetic mutations over 10-15 years and can transform into invasive adenocarcinoma. Early detection and removal of these polyps effectively prevents colorectal cancer.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 40-year-old man taking high-dose ibuprofen daily for chronic back pain presents with sharp epigastric pain that worsens with eating. Endoscopy reveals a gastric ulcer. His physician discontinues ibuprofen and starts a PPI. Why did ibuprofen cause this ulcer?

options.text: Ibuprofen directly burns the stomach lining on contact
Ibuprofen inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes, blocking prostaglandin synthesis, which reduces protective mucus secretion, bicarbonate production, and mucosal blood flow in the stomach
Ibuprofen stimulates parietal cells to produce excessive hydrochloric acid
Ibuprofen promotes H. pylori colonization by weakening the immune system

quiz_questions.explanation: NSAIDs like ibuprofen inhibit both COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes. COX-1 is constitutively expressed in the gastric mucosa and produces prostaglandins (PGE2, PGI2) that maintain mucosal defense by stimulating mucus and bicarbonate secretion, maintaining mucosal blood flow, and promoting epithelial cell turnover. Blocking these protective prostaglandins tips the balance toward acid-induced mucosal damage and ulcer formation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pediatrician evaluates a 4-year-old child with failure to thrive, chronic diarrhea, abdominal distension, and iron-deficiency anemia. The child's symptoms began after introduction of wheat-based cereals at 6 months of age. Blood tests reveal elevated anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) antibodies. The pediatrician orders a duodenal biopsy. What findings does she expect, and what treatment will she recommend?

options.text: Normal villi with H. pylori organisms; triple antibiotic therapy
Villous atrophy with crypt hyperplasia and intraepithelial lymphocytes, confirming celiac disease; treatment is a strict lifelong gluten-free diet
Transmural granulomas indicating Crohn's disease; treatment is immunosuppressive medication
Normal biopsy with no abnormalities; no treatment needed

quiz_questions.explanation: The clinical picture (failure to thrive after wheat introduction, chronic diarrhea, iron-deficiency anemia) and positive anti-tTG antibodies strongly suggest celiac disease. Duodenal biopsy confirming villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes is the gold standard for diagnosis. The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet, which allows the intestinal villi to regenerate and symptoms to resolve.
